title: Collapse during a startling alarm in a young woman on multiple new drugs
diagnosis: Long QT syndrome with torsades de pointes

chart.cc: Passed out and woke up on the floor
chart.oneLiner: 24F with anxiety/depression and a recent vomiting-and-diarrhea illness, on several new medications (an antibiotic, an SSRI, an antiemetic, an OTC antihistamine, and a thiazide for her blood pressure), presents after a brief loss of consciousness triggered by a sudden loud alarm, preceded by palpitations, with a family history of unexplained sudden death.

opening: A fire alarm went off, my heart started racing, and the next thing I knew I was waking up on the floor.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 24-year-old woman, now alert and back to baseline, anxious. Good historian.

HPI: Brief loss of consciousness immediately after a sudden LOUD fire alarm; felt a few seconds of palpitations/fluttering before going down. Witnessed brief generalized shaking for a few seconds, but recovered QUICKLY with NO post-event confusion, NO tongue biting, NO incontinence (favors convulsive syncope over a primary seizure). Two prior near-fainting spells — one while startled by a phone ringing, one during a swim. NO chest pain, NO true aura, NO focal weakness. For the past four days she has had a stomach bug with vomiting and loose stools and has been eating and drinking poorly (only reveal the GI illness/poor intake if asked about recent illness, diet, vomiting, or diarrhea — it is why her potassium and magnesium are low).
FAMILY HISTORY (only if asked about family/sudden death): a brother died suddenly in his sleep at age 19 (unexplained); a maternal cousin 'drowned' while swimming; no known deafness in the family.
PMH: occasional palpitations; anxiety and depression (recently started treatment); mildly high blood pressure her GP just began treating; otherwise healthy. No diabetes, no kidney disease.
MEDS (reveal the specifics only if asked about medications or new drugs — ALL of these can prolong the QT or drop her potassium/magnesium): azithromycin started 4 days ago for a sinus infection; an SSRI (citalopram) started about three weeks ago for low mood; ondansetron taken over the last few days for the vomiting; an over-the-counter antihistamine (diphenhydramine) for allergies; and hydrochlorothiazide started recently for blood pressure. NO recreational drugs. ALLERGIES: none.
SOCIAL: social alcohol only, non-smoker, no recreational/street drugs. Works as a teacher. Lives with a roommate. FAMILY: as above. ROS: palpitations as noted; no chest pain, no dyspnea, no weight change, no diarrhea/vomiting today (resolving).

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: alert, anxious-appearing, no distress; no dysmorphic features.
- Vitals: BP 108/68, HR 84 and regular, RR 16, 36.8°C, SpO2 99% RA; mildly dry mucous membranes after the GI illness.
- Cardiac: regular rate and rhythm, normal S1/S2, NO murmur, NO gallop; peripheral pulses normal and symmetric.
- Neuro: fully oriented, no focal deficit, no tongue laceration, normal gait.
- Mucous membranes: slightly dry; skin turgor mildly reduced (recent volume loss).
- Hearing: normal to conversation (no congenital deafness).

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the family history of sudden death/drowning unless asked about family history or sudden death in relatives.
- Do NOT volunteer the new antibiotic/SSRI/antiemetic/antihistamine/thiazide unless asked about medications or recent new drugs.
- Do NOT volunteer the recent vomiting-and-diarrhea illness or poor intake unless asked about recent illness, diet, or GI symptoms.
- Emphasize (only if asked about triggers/warning) that it happened right after a sudden loud noise and was preceded by palpitations.
- Reveal exam findings (e.g. dry mucous membranes) only when the relevant exam is performed.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.
- Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing.

labs.EKG_QTc: 12-lead EKG: sinus rhythm at ~82 bpm; QT interval markedly prolonged with a measured QTc of 530 ms (normal <460 ms in women) by Bazett; broad-based, notched T waves with prominent U waves. Diagnostic of QT prolongation and a substrate for torsades.
labs.Telemetry: Captured a self-terminating run of polymorphic VT with a twisting/undulating QRS axis around the baseline (torsades de pointes), initiated by a short-long-short (pause-dependent) sequence. No sustained arrhythmia at present; frequent PVCs.
labs.BMP_electrolytes: Na 139, K 2.9 mmol/L (LOW), Cl 96, HCO3 30 (HIGH) — anion gap = 139 − (96 + 30) = 13 (normal); a hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis from vomiting plus the thiazide. BUN 18, Cr 0.9 (normal renal function), glucose 92. Mg 1.3 mg/dL (LOW) and Ca 8.2 mg/dL (LOW, with normal albumin) — the low K, Mg, and Ca all further prolong the QT.
labs.Troponin: Negative x2 — no acute ischemic pattern to explain the arrhythmia.
labs.TSH: Normal — excludes thyroid contribution / acquired QT from hypothyroidism.
labs.CBC: Hgb 13.6, WBC 8.1, Plt 245 — no anemia or infection-related abnormality; mildly concentrated consistent with recent volume loss.
labs.Tox_screen: Urine drug screen negative — no stimulant/cocaine effect; supports a drug-therapeutic + electrolyte + congenital mechanism rather than illicit-drug toxicity.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Sinus rhythm at ~82 bpm with a markedly prolonged QT interval (QTc ~530 ms), broad-based notched T waves and prominent U waves — drug- and electrolyte-aggravated long QT, the substrate for torsades de pointes.
ekg.verify: From the LITFL 'QT interval / Long QT syndrome' library page — use the drug-induced long QT example tracing that matches: sinus rhythm with a markedly prolonged QT interval (QTc ~530 ms), broad/notched T waves and U waves. Self-host with credit to litfl.com; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.

ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead EKG showing a markedly prolonged QT interval with broad notched T waves and U waves (drug- and electrolyte-aggravated long QT)
ekg.clinicalVignette: A 24-year-old woman on several new QT-prolonging drugs presents after a sudden fainting episode preceded by palpitations.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Normal cardiac silhouette and clear lung fields; no cardiomegaly, effusion, or acute process (a normal film is itself a meaningful read in primary electrical disease).
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: normal PA chest radiograph with a normal cardiac silhouette and clear lungs. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
cxr.clinicalVignette: A young woman with recurrent unexplained fainting has a chest film to exclude structural disease.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential for this syncope (note your leading diagnosis). As part of your answer, name three causes or mechanisms of the leading diagnosis you are proposing.
steps.rubric: Cardiac syncope due to a ventricular arrhythmia from a prolonged QT / torsades (leading & CANT-MISS,8). Other inherited arrhythmia / structural cardiac cause — HCM, Brugada, catecholaminergic VT (CANT-MISS,4). Seizure / epilepsy (CANT-MISS,3). Names three causes/mechanisms of QT prolongation — e.g. QT-prolonging DRUGS (azithromycin, citalopram, ondansetron, antihistamine), ELECTROLYTES (low K/Mg/Ca), and a CONGENITAL channelopathy (LQTS) (core,4). Recognizes the central fork — congenital LQTS vs acquired (drug + electrolyte) QT prolongation — and that the two coexist here (core,4). Vasovagal syncope (core,2). PENALTY: anchoring purely on a benign vasovagal/anxiety faint and ignoring the cardiac/arrhythmic cause (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Cardiac syncope from a ventricular arrhythmia due to a prolonged QT interval (torsades de pointes). The key fork is CONGENITAL long QT syndrome (channelopathy) vs ACQUIRED QT prolongation from QT-prolonging drugs and electrolyte loss — and here they coexist: she has the congenital triggers (startle/auditory and swimming, family sudden death) AND multiple acquired hits (azithromycin, citalopram, ondansetron, an antihistamine, a thiazide, plus a vomiting/diarrhea illness causing hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia). Three causes/mechanisms of QT prolongation: (1) QT-prolonging DRUGS, (2) ELECTROLYTE derangement (low K, Mg, Ca), (3) CONGENITAL ion-channel mutation (LQTS); other accepted answers include bradycardia/pauses, hypothyroidism, hypothermia. Must-not-miss alternatives to exclude: other inherited arrhythmia/structural disease (HCM, Brugada, CPVT) and Seizure/epilepsy. Also reasonable: Vasovagal syncope.

criticalActions.item: Cardiac syncope from ventricular arrhythmia due to prolonged QT / torsades de pointes
Other inherited arrhythmia / structural cardiac disease (HCM, Brugada, CPVT)

coreActions.item: Names three causes/mechanisms of QT prolongation (e.g. QT-prolonging drugs, electrolyte derangement, congenital channelopathy)
Recognizes the congenital LQTS vs acquired (drug + electrolyte) QT-prolongation fork and that both contribute here

penalties.item: Anchoring on a benign vasovagal/anxiety faint and ignoring the cardiac/arrhythmic cause

mustNotMiss: Other inherited arrhythmia / structural cardiac disease (HCM, Brugada, CPVT)

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: 12-lead EKG immediately with measured/corrected QT (CANT-MISS — diagnostic,6). Continuous telemetry / cardiac monitoring to capture arrhythmia (CANT-MISS,4). BMP with magnesium and calcium (electrolytes that prolong QT) (2). Review and STOP every QT-prolonging / potassium-wasting medication on her list (2). TSH, CBC, troponin, and a urine tox screen to complete the acquired-cause search (1). PENALTY: discharging or failing to monitor a patient with syncope and palpitations (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: 12-lead EKG immediately with a measured QTc; continuous telemetry / cardiac monitoring; BMP with magnesium and calcium; review and STOP all QT-prolonging and K-wasting drugs (azithromycin, citalopram, ondansetron, diphenhydramine, hydrochlorothiazide); troponin; TSH; CBC; urine tox screen; and a CXR to exclude structural disease.

coreActions.item: Review and stop all QT-prolonging / potassium-wasting medications
TSH, CBC, troponin, and urine tox screen

penalties.item: discharging or failing to monitor a patient with syncope and palpitations

optionalTests: Review and stop all QT-prolonging / potassium-wasting medications
TSH, CBC, troponin, and urine tox screen
contraindicatedTests: discharging or failing to monitor a patient with syncope and palpitations

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's own 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings (be specific about the intervals) and state what it commits you to.
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus rhythm with a markedly prolonged QT interval (QTc ~530 ms), broad notched T waves and prominent U waves — the substrate for torsades, here aggravated by her QT-prolonging drugs and by hypokalemia/hypomagnesemia. CREDIT: measures/identifies the QT and recognizes it is markedly prolonged (6); corrects for rate (QTc) rather than raw QT (4); notes the broad/notched T waves with U waves in sinus rhythm and links them to low K/Mg and QT-prolonging drugs (3); systematic read with rhythm/rate/axis/intervals (2). PENALTY: calling the tracing normal or missing the long QT (-3).
keyFindings: markedly prolonged QT interval with a QTc ~530 ms
corrects QT for rate (QTc) rather than reading the raw QT
broad notched T waves with prominent U waves in sinus rhythm
links the long QT to QT-prolonging drugs and hypokalemia/hypomagnesemia
criticalFindings: Sinus rhythm with a markedly prolonged QT interval (QTc ~530 ms), broad notched T waves and prominent U waves — the substrate for torsades de pointes, aggravated by QT-prolonging drugs and hypokalemia/hypomagnesemia.
commonMistakes: reading the raw QT without correcting for heart rate (QTc)
missing the U waves of hypokalemia
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus rhythm at ~82 bpm with a markedly prolonged QT interval (QTc ~530 ms), broad notched T waves and prominent U waves. This is long QT — the substrate for torsades de pointes — and it is being aggravated by her QT-prolonging medications and by hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia. It commits me to continuous monitoring, stopping every QT-prolonging drug, and repleting K and Mg.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: sinus rhythm with a markedly prolonged QT interval (QTc ~530 ms), broad notched T waves and U waves — the substrate for torsades de pointes.

coreActions.item: identifies a markedly prolonged QT interval
corrects QT for rate (QTc) rather than reading the raw QT
notes broad notched T waves and U waves in sinus rhythm and links them to low K/Mg and QT-prolonging drugs

penalties.item: calling the tracing normal or missing the long QT

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = normal film. CREDIT: identifies a normal/near-normal film (6); notes the normal heart size helps exclude structural disease/cardiomyopathy as a cause (4). PENALTY: inventing pathology (-2).
keyFindings: notes normal heart size helps exclude structural cardiac disease
criticalFindings: Normal cardiac silhouette and clear lungs — no acute cardiopulmonary process.
commonMistakes: inventing cardiomegaly or an effusion on a normal film
steps.idealAnswer: Normal chest radiograph with a normal cardiac silhouette and clear lungs; supports a primary electrical (channelopathy) plus acquired cause rather than structural disease.

criticalActions: Normal cardiac silhouette and clear lungs — no acute cardiopulmonary process.
coreActions: notes normal heart size helps exclude structural cardiac disease

penalties.label: invents pathology on a normal film

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Her own EKG shows a QTc of 530 ms with U waves, telemetry captured a pause-dependent self-terminating run of polymorphic VT with a twisting axis, the BMP shows K 2.9 and Mg 1.3 with a metabolic alkalosis, and she has a family history of sudden death plus a list of new QT-prolonging drugs. Commit to a single diagnosis, say how you would settle the congenital-vs-acquired question, and explain why it is dangerous.
steps.rubric: Long QT syndrome with torsades de pointes — committing despite the ambiguity (6). Resolves the fork: this is most likely a CONGENITAL long QT predisposition UNMASKED by acquired hits — the startle/auditory and swimming triggers, prior near-faints, and family sudden death point to an inherited channelopathy, while the new QT-prolonging drugs and hypokalemia/hypomagnesemia were the precipitants; the discriminating step is to STOP the offending drugs, correct the electrolytes, then RE-MEASURE the QTc (a QTc that stays prolonged off drugs and with normal K/Mg argues congenital and warrants genetic testing/EP referral and family screening) (2). Explains the danger — torsades can degenerate into ventricular fibrillation and cause sudden cardiac death; low K/Mg and the drugs potentiate it (2).
steps.idealAnswer: Long QT syndrome with torsades de pointes — I commit to this despite the residual ambiguity. The most coherent story is a CONGENITAL long QT predisposition UNMASKED by acquired hits: the auditory-startle and swimming triggers, the recurrent near-faints, and the family history of sudden death and a drowning point to an inherited channelopathy, while the recently started QT-prolonging drugs (azithromycin, citalopram, ondansetron, antihistamine) and the hypokalemia/hypomagnesemia from her vomiting illness and thiazide were the precipitants that tipped her into torsades. The discriminating step is to stop every offending drug, fully correct K and Mg, and then RE-MEASURE the QTc on telemetry: a QTc that stays prolonged once she is off the drugs with normal electrolytes argues for congenital LQTS and warrants EP referral, genetic testing, and screening of first-degree relatives. It is dangerous because torsades can degenerate into ventricular fibrillation and sudden cardiac death, and it is potentiated by the QT-prolonging drugs and by hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia, which must be corrected and avoided.
mustNotMiss: Degeneration of torsades into ventricular fibrillation / sudden cardiac death
acceptableAlternatives: Congenital long QT syndrome unmasked by QT-prolonging drugs and hypokalemia/hypomagnesemia
Acquired (drug + electrolyte) long QT precipitating torsades

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan, including the acute treatment, the medication reconciliation, and your disposition / safe-discharge decision?
steps.rubric: Per MGH torsades / long QT: IV magnesium sulfate for torsades regardless of serum level (CANT-MISS,6); stop ALL QT-prolonging and K-wasting drugs — azithromycin, citalopram, ondansetron, antihistamine, hydrochlorothiazide (CANT-MISS,5); aggressively replete potassium (target high-normal) and magnesium (4); continuous telemetry on a monitored bed and increase heart rate to shorten QT — isoproterenol or overdrive/temporary pacing for recurrent pause-dependent torsades (3); cardiology/EP consult for beta-blocker and ICD evaluation plus genetic testing in congenital LQTS (2); DISPOSITION — admit to a monitored bed and do NOT discharge; arrange safe follow-up: documented drug-allergy/avoid-QT-drug list, an up-to-date 'do-not-use' medication card/MedicAlert, family/first-degree-relative screening and counseling, and close cardiology/EP follow-up (3); unsynchronized defibrillation if torsades becomes sustained/unstable (1). PENALTY: giving a QT-prolonging antiarrhythmic such as amiodarone/sotalol/procainamide (-3); discharging the patient home (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Acute: give IV magnesium sulfate first-line for torsades even if the magnesium level is normal; immediately STOP every QT-prolonging and potassium-wasting drug on her list (azithromycin, citalopram, ondansetron, diphenhydramine, hydrochlorothiazide) and aggressively replete potassium to a high-normal level and magnesium. Keep her on continuous telemetry in a monitored bed; for recurrent pause-dependent torsades increase the heart rate with isoproterenol or temporary overdrive pacing, and if torsades becomes sustained or hemodynamically unstable perform unsynchronized defibrillation. Avoid all QT-prolonging agents (including amiodarone and sotalol). Cardiology/EP consult for a beta-blocker, ICD consideration, and genetic testing for congenital long QT. DISPOSITION / safe discharge: she is NOT safe to go home now — admit to a monitored bed. Before any eventual discharge: complete medication reconciliation with a documented 'avoid QT-prolonging drugs' list and a MedicAlert/wallet card, counsel her to avoid the offending drugs and to correct electrolytes promptly during future illnesses, arrange screening EKGs and EP follow-up, and screen and counsel her first-degree relatives given the family sudden-death history.

criticalActions.item: Stop all QT-prolonging and potassium-wasting drugs

coreActions.item: Continuous telemetry and increase heart rate (isoproterenol / overdrive pacing) for pause-dependent torsades
Cardiology/EP consult for beta-blocker, ICD evaluation, and genetic testing
Admit to a monitored bed; safe-discharge planning — med reconciliation, avoid-QT-drug list/MedicAlert, family screening, close EP follow-up

bonusActions.item: Unsynchronized defibrillation if torsades becomes sustained/unstable

requiredActions: Stop all QT-prolonging and potassium-wasting drugs
optionalActions: Continuous telemetry and increase heart rate (isoproterenol / overdrive pacing) for pause-dependent torsades
Cardiology/EP consult for beta-blocker, ICD evaluation, and genetic testing
Admit to a monitored bed; safe-discharge planning — med reconciliation, avoid-QT-drug list/MedicAlert, family screening, close EP follow-up
Unsynchronized defibrillation if torsades becomes sustained/unstable

caseSummary.finalDiagnosis: Long QT syndrome with torsades de pointes
caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 24F with anxiety/depression, newly treated mild hypertension, and a recent vomiting-and-diarrhea illness — on azithromycin, citalopram, ondansetron, an OTC antihistamine, and hydrochlorothiazide — presents after a brief loss of consciousness triggered by a sudden loud alarm, preceded by palpitations, with a family history of unexplained sudden death. The diagnosis is Long QT syndrome with torsades de pointes: a congenital long QT predisposition unmasked by multiple acquired hits (QT-prolonging drugs and hypokalemia/hypomagnesemia). It is reached by eliciting the focused history (family sudden death, the full medication list, the GI illness), performing the targeted exam, interpreting her OWN long-QT EKG and the telemetry, integrating the multi-system electrolyte derangements, and excluding the must-not-miss alternatives (other channelopathy/structural disease, seizure).
keyHistoryClues: Syncope triggered by a sudden loud noise (startle), preceded by palpitations.
Prior near-faints with auditory startle and during swimming (LQT2/LQT1 triggers).
Family history of unexplained sudden death and a relative who drowned while swimming.
Five new QT-prolonging or potassium-wasting drugs: azithromycin, citalopram, ondansetron, antihistamine, hydrochlorothiazide.
Recent vomiting-and-diarrhea illness with poor intake driving hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia.
Brief convulsive movements but rapid recovery without post-ictal confusion, tongue biting, or incontinence.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Cardiac: regular rate and rhythm, normal S1/S2, NO murmur, NO gallop.
Neuro: fully oriented, no focal deficit, no tongue laceration, normal gait.
Mucous membranes slightly dry; skin turgor mildly reduced — consistent with recent volume loss.
Cardiac: peripheral pulses normal and symmetric.
General: alert, anxious-appearing, no distress; no dysmorphic features.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items (family sudden death, the full new-medication list, the recent GI illness) only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings, including signs of recent volume loss.
Build a differential led by Long QT syndrome with torsades, naming three causes/mechanisms of QT prolongation and the congenital-vs-acquired fork, while excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused workup; interpret the patient's OWN long-QT EKG and telemetry and integrate the multi-system electrolyte derangements.

Commit to Long QT syndrome with torsades de pointes, resolve the congenital-vs-acquired question by stopping drugs / correcting electrolytes / re-measuring the QTc, and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, family screening, and safe disposition.
commonPitfalls: Mislabeling convulsive syncope as a primary seizure and discharging without telemetry
Reading the raw QT without correcting for heart rate (QTc)
Missing one or more of the five QT-prolonging / potassium-wasting drugs
Discharging the patient home instead of admitting to a monitored bed
managementPearls: IV magnesium sulfate for torsades regardless of serum level
Stop ALL QT-prolonging and potassium-wasting drugs
Replete potassium to high-normal and magnesium; increase heart rate for pause-dependent torsades
Admit to a monitored bed; arrange med reconciliation, avoid-QT-drug card, and family screening before any discharge

imageInterpretationPearls: A normal film is an interpretation task in its own right — a normal heart size supports a primary electrical cause over structural disease.
Always correct the QT for heart rate (QTc) before calling it normal or prolonged, and look for the U waves of hypokalemia.
Read every study systematically and state pertinent negatives, not just positives.
